archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style

selected_question.question_text: You're scrolling through Instagram and a body wash ad catches your eye. It's a 30-second cinematic reel — a guy your age walking through Mumbai streets after rain, slow-motion water droplets, moody lighting, great background score, and the tagline 'Feel Alive.' No ingredient list, no claims, no percentages. Just vibes. What's your honest gut reaction, and what do you do next?
selected_question.why_selected: None of the 291 questions in the bank directly probe ad processing style — they all focus on purchase decision mechanics (pricing, deals, channel choice). This generated question places the person in the exact scenario described in the dimension definition: an ad interrupts their feed, and we observe whether they engage emotionally (save it, look up the brand based on the vibe) or analytically (dismiss it for lacking substance). It's calibrated to this person specifically because he doom-scrolls Instagram (Q56), discovers products there (Q54), values both aesthetic content AND ingredient breakdowns (Q59), and had a mixed experience with Bath & Body Works where aesthetics drew him in but weak claims disappointed him (Q23). This question forces him to reveal which cognitive mode fires first when no analytical hooks are present — pure emotional stimulus only.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, I'd probably save that reel. If the visuals are that good and the vibe feels premium and masculine, it sticks with me. I'd tap on the brand profile, scroll through their grid to see if the aesthetic is consistent, and maybe add it to my mental list of things to check out next time I'm on Blinkit. The mood of the ad matters more than a claims dump — if it feels like 'me,' I'll do the research later on my own terms.
archetypes.why_plausible: He was drawn into Bath & Body Works purely by the aesthetic mall store experience (Q23). He values aesthetic content as a top-2 content type (Q59). His Plum discovery started with heavy visual social media campaigns before any research (Q58). He engages with influencers based on whether they 'look good' (Q57). This suggests a plausible mode where visuals and vibe are the initial hook that triggers downstream action.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: When shown a body wash concept, this twin would first react to the overall brand identity, packaging design, and whether it feels premium and masculine. A concept with a strong visual story and aspirational positioning but average ingredient list would score well. A concept with excellent ingredients but clinical, sterile presentation would feel uninspiring and get lower initial interest scores.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: A cinematic, mood-driven ad with strong music and lifestyle imagery would generate high engagement — saves, profile visits, brand recall. A data-heavy ad leading with '87% reduced oiliness' would feel cold and be scrolled past. This twin would respond best to influencer content that's visually polished and aspirational rather than educational.

archetypes.answer_text: The ad would make me pause — good visuals always do. But within a few seconds I'd be thinking 'okay cool, but what does it actually do?' I'd probably check the comments to see if anyone mentions ingredients or if the brand replied with details. If the brand profile has no substance — just aesthetic posts with no ingredient info or dermatologist backing — I'd move on. The vibe gets my attention, but I need something real to actually consider buying it.
archetypes.why_plausible: This mirrors his actual Plum journey (Q58): visual campaigns caught his attention, but he didn't buy until he saw it on Blinkit and did further research. His Bath & Body Works experience (Q23) shows he's drawn by aesthetics but then evaluates claims critically and was disappointed when they didn't hold up. He ranks expert opinion and ingredients as top factors (Q11) but also values aesthetic content (Q59). This dual-mode processing is the most consistent reading of his full profile.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would engage with concepts that balance strong visual identity with credible claims. A concept needs to pass both the 'does this feel like me' test AND the 'does this actually work' test. Concepts with great branding but vague claims would generate initial interest but fail at conversion. Concepts with strong clinical backing AND appealing design would score highest.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: A cinematic ad would earn a pause and a profile click, but conversion requires a follow-up touchpoint with substance — an ingredient breakdown in the brand's bio, a dermatologist collab post, or visible on quick commerce with good ratings. A purely data-driven ad would be respected but not emotionally engaging enough to stop the scroll. Best-performing ads for this twin combine mood with a single credible claim.

archetypes.answer_text: It looks nice, sure, but I'd scroll past. A 30-second ad with zero information about what's in it or what it does for my skin is basically wasted on me. I have oily, acne-prone skin — I need to know if this thing is sulphate-free, what actives it has, whether it's been tested. 'Feel Alive' tells me nothing. I'd rather see a boring-looking ad that breaks down the formulation than a cinematic one that's all vibes and no substance.
archetypes.why_plausible: He ranks expert opinion and ingredients as his #1 and #2 purchase factors (Q11). He trusts dermatologists most (5/5) and product packaging claims least (1/5) (Q18). He used ChatGPT to research a simplified skincare routine (Q34) and specifically values clinical-grade formulations enough to pay a premium (Q16). His frustration with Bath & Body Works' unsubstantiated 3-in-1 claim (Q23) and his ideal body wash description focusing on functional benefits (Q49) suggest he could default to pure analytical mode when ads lack substance.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would immediately scan a concept for ingredient list, clinical backing, and specific skin benefit claims. A concept positioned around 'targets oiliness and dark spots, sulphate-free, clinically tested' with plain packaging would outperform a beautifully designed concept with vague 'nourishing' claims. Would flag inconsistencies between claims and ingredient lists and penalize concepts that feel like marketing fluff.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: A data-led ad showing clinical study results, ingredient callouts, or a dermatologist endorsement would generate the highest engagement and purchase intent. A cinematic lifestyle ad would be dismissed as 'all packaging, no product.' Influencer content would only work if the influencer does an actual ingredient breakdown or comparison. This twin would be most likely to convert from a YouTube long-form review rather than an Instagram reel.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Social Identity Signaling Through Product Choices

selected_question.question_text: You've been using a ₹199 body wash for a year and are satisfied. A colleague at work casually mentions your body wash is 'the budget option' and recommends their ₹499 one. How does this affect your next purchase? A) I'd feel no change — I'm happy with what works B) I'd be curious and look up the ₹499 option C) I'd probably switch to avoid being seen as using a 'budget' product D) I'd feel annoyed at the comment but might quietly research alternatives
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes whether social perception of one's product choice triggers a behavioral change — the core of identity signaling. It creates a realistic peer-judgment scenario that separates people who are functionally driven (choose A), socially curious but not threatened (choose B/D), and those for whom brand perception is a social identity concern (choose C). For this specific person, it's ideal because he already uses Dettol (a mass/budget-coded brand) daily while having experimented with premium Bath & Body Works, and his colleague/peer context as a student in affluent Bombay makes the social pressure scenario highly realistic. His response would reveal whether the Bath & Body Works experiment was aspirational signaling or pure curiosity.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd feel no change honestly. I use Dettol lemon and it does the job — cleans well, available on Blinkit in 10 minutes. If someone thinks that's 'budget,' that's their problem. I'd rather spend that ₹499 on a good face serum that actually makes a visible difference to my skin. I don't need my body wash to impress anyone.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with his low brand loyalty (Q17), his pragmatic reliance on Blinkit convenience (Q25, Q44), his willingness to pay premium only for clinical-grade formulations with proven results (Q16), and his dissatisfaction with Bath & Body Works despite its premium positioning (Q23, Q51). He already uses Dettol without apparent embarrassment and ranks expert opinion and ingredients far above brand name (Q11).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would dismiss a body wash positioned as a 'status' or 'insider' brand with Instagram-worthy packaging. Would evaluate purely on ingredients, clinical claims, and whether it addresses oiliness/acne/dark spots. A concept with 'curated for those who know' positioning would score low — he'd find it pretentious and check if the formulation justifies the price.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: An ad showing a product in a stylish bathroom or a peer-compliment scene ('what body wash do you use?') would feel irrelevant or cringe. Would respond strongly to an ad featuring a dermatologist explaining ingredient science, before-after skin results, or a breakdown of how the formulation tackles Indian climate-specific concerns like oiliness and sweat.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd probably look up the ₹499 option that night — not because I feel judged, but the comment would make me think maybe I'm missing out on something better. I'd check the ingredients and reviews, and if it genuinely seems like an upgrade for my skin, I'd try it. But if it's just fancy packaging and fragrance, I'd stick with what I have. I wouldn't want to be seen as someone who doesn't care about what they use, but I also won't switch just for the brand name.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with his openness to trying new brands (Q7: 5/5), his pattern of discovering products through social exposure then researching independently (Q58 — Plum discovery), and his Bath & Body Works experiment driven partly by the premium mall experience (Q23). He's not brand-loyal but is clearly influenced by exposure and peer context. His family environment where product choices are noticed (Q32) suggests some ambient awareness of how choices are perceived.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would engage with a premium D2C body wash concept if it combines aesthetic positioning WITH credible ingredient/clinical claims. Would notice and appreciate Instagram-worthy packaging but wouldn't convert unless the formulation story is strong. A concept that balances 'elevated taste' with 'science-backed' would score highest — pure status positioning without substance would lose him.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would respond to an ad that shows a relatable young guy in a metro context using the product as part of a put-together lifestyle — but only if the ad also includes an ingredient callout or dermatologist endorsement. A peer-compliment scene would catch his attention and create curiosity, but he'd need a second touchpoint (ingredient breakdown, influencer review) to convert. Pure lifestyle ads without substance would generate interest but not purchase intent.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd feel a bit annoyed at the comment but honestly it would get to me. I'd definitely look up the ₹499 option and probably try it. I already know my Dettol smells like a utensil cleanser — I've said it myself. If I'm spending time on skincare and grooming, the body wash should match that effort. I'd want something in my bathroom that reflects the kind of person I'm becoming, not just something that's convenient on Blinkit.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with his self-description as 'passionate and smart' (Q6), his detailed ideal grooming routine with multiple touchpoints (Q10), his attraction to the Bath & Body Works premium mall experience (Q23), and his own frustration that Dettol smells like a utensil cleanser (Q45). His ideal body wash includes 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' and 'minimalist packaging' (Q49) — both aesthetic/identity-laden preferences. His high comfort trying new brands (Q7) and discovery through Instagram (Q54) suggest receptivity to aspirational positioning. His affluent household (Q3: >₹2L/month) means premium products are financially accessible.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond very positively to a premium D2C body wash with distinctive minimalist packaging, a 'curated for discerning men' positioning, and a masculine fragrance profile. Would imagine the bottle in his bathroom and consider how it fits his evolving grooming identity. Would rate high purchase intent even at ₹450-500 if the brand story signals taste and sophistication alongside credible ingredients.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would strongly resonate with an ad showing a well-groomed young man in a stylish Bombay apartment, product visible in the bathroom, with a peer or date noticing his scent. Lifestyle-forward content with subtle ingredient credibility cues would be the sweet spot. Influencer content from someone who 'looks good and has good skin' (Q57) using the product in an aspirational but relatable context would drive trial. Would share or save such an ad if it felt like it reflected his self-image.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Tolerance for Aspirational vs Authentic Representation

selected_question.question_text: You're scrolling Instagram and see two ads for a new body wash from the same brand. Ad A shows a guy in a sleek, marble-tiled bathroom with dramatic lighting, slow-motion water cascading off his shoulders, and a deep voiceover saying 'Elevate your ritual.' Ad B shows a regular guy in a typical Mumbai apartment bathroom, natural lighting, real skin texture visible, casually talking to the camera about how the body wash helped with his oily skin after a long commute. Which ad makes you more likely to tap and explore the product, and why?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the aspirational-vs-authentic tension that is genuinely ambiguous in this person's profile. He values relatable influencers ('similar location context and income level' — Q55) and was drawn to ingredient breakdowns (Q59), yet he was also pulled into Bath & Body Works by the premium aesthetic store experience (Q23) and explicitly values 'aesthetic content' (Q59). None of the 291 bank questions address visual/narrative register in advertising — they are all purchase-decision-mechanics questions about pricing, deals, and channel behavior. This generated question isolates the exact dimension: does a polished aspirational frame or a grounded authentic frame trigger more engagement for this specific person, in a body wash advertising context he'd realistically encounter on Instagram (his primary discovery channel per Q54).

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, Ad A would catch my eye first. The marble bathroom, the lighting, the whole vibe — it just makes the product feel premium and worth exploring. I know it's marketing, but if a brand can present itself that well, it signals they've put effort into the product too. I'd tap on it, check the ingredients, and then decide. The regular bathroom ad feels a bit too basic to stop my scroll.
archetypes.why_plausible: He was drawn to Bath & Body Works' premium mall aesthetic (Q23), explicitly values 'aesthetic content' as a top content type (Q59), prefers minimalist/sleek packaging (Q41, Q49), and his ideal body wash fragrance reference is Nautica perfume — a luxury brand (Q42). His high household income (>₹2L/month) means aspirational settings aren't alien to his lived reality. He also rated Plum and Dot & Key highly (Q48) — brands known for premium visual identity.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a body wash concept with aspirational imagery (premium bottle, international aesthetic, model with flawless skin) significantly higher on perceived quality and purchase intent. Would see minimalist-luxe packaging as a signal of clinical-grade formulation — his top willingness-to-pay-premium category (Q16). Would be less engaged by concepts using 'real user' photography or everyday settings.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage more with glossy, highly produced video ads — slow-motion, cinematic color grading, premium settings. Would perceive such ads as signaling product quality and brand seriousness. Would scroll past ads with low production value or 'UGC-style' footage, categorizing them as budget brands. Influencer ads would need to feature creators with polished aesthetics, not raw/unfiltered content.

archetypes.answer_text: Ad B, no question. The marble bathroom ad feels like every generic men's body wash commercial — all vibes, no substance. The Mumbai apartment guy talking about oily skin after a commute? That's literally my life. I'd actually trust that he's used the product. I'd want to hear what ingredients helped and whether the results lasted. Ad A would make me think they spent more on the ad than on the formulation.
archetypes.why_plausible: He explicitly requires influencer relatability — 'similar location context and income level' (Q55). He distrusts product packaging claims (rated 1/5 in Q18) and TV ads (2/5). He was disappointed by Bath & Body Works despite the premium experience — the 3-in-1 claim didn't hold up (Q23), showing he penalizes brands that over-promise visually. He values expert opinion and ingredients over brand and packaging (Q11). He used ChatGPT for a simplified routine rather than following aspirational dermatologist content (Q34), suggesting he values practical substance over polished presentation.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would be skeptical of body wash concepts with aspirational imagery, questioning whether the brand invested in formulation or just marketing. Would respond much better to concepts foregrounding ingredient lists, clinical study data, and India-specific claims (Q50). Would rate a 'real skin, real results' concept with before/after photos higher than a lifestyle-driven concept.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would dismiss glossy, cinematic ads as 'all marketing, no substance' and actively distrust the product. Would engage with ads featuring ingredient breakdowns, dermatologist explanations, or relatable scenarios (sweating in Mumbai heat, oily skin after commute). UGC-style or testimonial ads from regular guys would drive higher click-through and purchase consideration.

archetypes.answer_text: Ad A would stop my scroll — the visuals are clean and the production quality signals the brand is serious. But I wouldn't buy from that alone. I'd tap on it hoping to find ingredient details, clinical backing, or something that tells me the product actually works for oily skin. If Ad A leads to a page with just vibes and no substance, I'd bounce. Ad B is more trustworthy but I might not even notice it while scrolling. Ideally, give me Ad A's look with Ad B's honesty.
archetypes.why_plausible: This perfectly mirrors his actual behavior pattern: he was drawn to Bath & Body Works by the aesthetic store experience (Q23) but then critically evaluated the product claims and was disappointed (Q23, Q51). His top content types are 'ingredient breakdown' AND 'aesthetic content' (Q59) — he literally wants both. He browses on Instagram (Q54) where aesthetic content stops the scroll, but his purchase decision is driven by ingredients and expert opinion (Q11). His Plum discovery followed this exact funnel: noticed via social media aesthetics → researched on Blinkit → bought after reading more (Q58).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would initially engage with aspirational concept imagery but would quickly look for ingredient lists, clinical claims, and India-specific formulation details. A concept that combines premium visual design with transparent, substantive product information would score highest. A purely aspirational concept without ingredient depth would lose him at the consideration stage; a purely authentic concept might not capture initial attention.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would be captured by high-production-value ads but would only convert if the ad transitions into substantive content — ingredient callouts, dermatologist endorsement, or specific skin benefit claims. A two-part funnel (aesthetic video ad → detailed landing page with clinical data) would be most effective. Would disengage from ads that are all aesthetic with no informational payoff, and might miss low-production ads entirely despite finding them trustworthy if encountered.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Decision Urgency and Deliberation Window Length

selected_question.question_text: A body wash brand you've never tried sends you a ₹150 discount coupon valid for 48 hours on their website. Their body wash costs ₹399 (so you'd pay ₹249). Your current body wash still has about 2 weeks of use left. Do you: A) Use the coupon now — great deal, I'll stock up B) Let it expire — I don't need body wash right now C) Browse their site but probably won't buy unless something really stands out
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly isolates the deliberation window by introducing a time-pressured offer (48 hours) for a brand-new product when there's no immediate need. It separates short-deliberators (who'd act on the deal quickly), long-deliberators (who'd let it expire because they haven't had time to research), and context-dependent deliberators (who'd browse but need something compelling to override their default research process). Crucially, it mirrors this person's real dual-mode behavior: his Plum journey (Q58) was multi-touchpoint and slow, but his Blinkit replenishment (Q25) is impulsive. This scenario sits in the ambiguous middle — it's a new brand (triggers deliberation mode) but with a time constraint and a good price (triggers urgency mode). His answer would reveal which mode dominates when the two conflict, which is exactly what we can't infer from his existing 59 answers.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd probably use the coupon — ₹249 for a ₹399 body wash is a solid deal, and I'm always open to trying new brands anyway. I'd quickly check their Instagram page and ingredient list, and if it looks legit and has clinical-grade claims, I'd just go for it. Worst case, I have a backup body wash. I don't like letting good deals expire when I know I'll need the product eventually.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with his 5/5 comfort trying new brands (Q7), low brand loyalty (Q17), willingness to experiment (Q23 — tried Bath & Body Works out of curiosity), and his price-conscious but deal-responsive nature (Q21, Q26). His ₹350-500 usual spend (Q21) means ₹249 feels like a steal. His quick-commerce habit (Q25) shows he's comfortable with fast, low-deliberation purchases.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would give decisive, polarized concept scores — a quick 4/5 or 5/5 for concepts that hit his key triggers (clinical formulation, oil control, good fragrance) and a fast 1/5 or 2/5 for concepts that don't. Purchase intent would be high and immediate but may not convert if the product isn't available on quick commerce platforms at the moment of need. Would respond strongly to 'limited edition' or 'first 500 customers' framing in concept descriptions.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would respond well to ads with clear CTAs, swipe-up links, and urgency cues ('selling fast,' 'limited stock'). Instagram story ads with a direct Blinkit/website link would drive immediate clicks. Brand-awareness-only ads without a purchase path would feel incomplete and forgettable. Would engage with influencer content that includes discount codes with expiry dates.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd browse their site to check ingredients and see if they have any clinical backing, but honestly I probably wouldn't buy. 48 hours isn't enough time for me to feel confident about a brand I've never heard of. I'd rather wait until I see it come up again — maybe on an influencer's page or on Blinkit — and then do proper research. I still have two weeks of body wash left, so there's no rush.
archetypes.why_plausible: Mirrors his Plum purchase journey (Q58) — saw it on social media, then on Blinkit, then researched, then bought. His trust hierarchy (Q18) prioritizes dermatologists and friends over ads. His ideal body wash demands specific criteria (Q49) — sulphate-free, clinical-grade, dark spot targeting — suggesting he wouldn't commit without verifying claims. His ranking of expert opinion and ingredients above all else (Q11) means he needs time to validate. He also uses ChatGPT for research (Q34), indicating a deliberate information-gathering habit.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would give moderate scores (3/5) to most concepts, even ones he'd eventually buy. Would frequently select 'I'd look into it more' over 'I'd definitely buy.' His concept ratings would underpredict actual conversion — a 3/5 from this twin could convert at the same rate as a 4/5 from Twin A. Would ask follow-up questions about ingredients and clinical evidence before committing. Concepts with detailed ingredient breakdowns and dermatologist endorsements would score incrementally higher.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would respond best to multi-touchpoint campaigns — an initial awareness ad followed by retargeting with ingredient deep-dives and expert testimonials. Urgency-based ads ('48-hour sale,' 'limited edition') would feel manipulative and reduce trust. Seed-planting ads with memorable aesthetics and no hard CTA would perform well because they enter his consideration set for later research. Influencer content showing long-term usage results (before/after over weeks) would be more persuasive than single-use reviews.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: Context-dependent — short for replenishment, long for new brands, but deal pressure can compress the new-brand window
archetypes.answer_text: I'd definitely browse their site — check the ingredients, see if it's sulphate-free, look at what skin concerns it targets. If the ingredient list looks solid and it has some clinical backing, I might actually buy it at ₹249 because that's a good price point for me. But if it's just another generic 'natural' body wash with no real differentiation, I'd let the coupon expire. The 48-hour window wouldn't stress me — it's more about whether the product itself convinces me in those few minutes of browsing.
archetypes.why_plausible: This captures both his modes: the impulsive Blinkit buyer (Q25) and the careful Plum researcher (Q58). His willingness to try Bath & Body Works in-store (Q23) shows he can make faster decisions when the product is physically present or the deal is compelling. But his disappointment with that purchase shows he still evaluates critically. His ₹300-350 ideal price (Q49) means ₹249 is attractive enough to compress his deliberation window — but only if the product meets his ingredient and formulation standards (Q11, Q12, Q13). He's not time-pressured; he's quality-pressured.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin's concept scores would be bimodal — high (4-5/5) for concepts that immediately signal clinical credibility and ingredient transparency, and low (1-2/5) for everything else, with very few moderate scores. The speed of his decision depends on information density in the concept — a concept with a clear ingredient list and dermatologist endorsement would get a fast, confident high score. A concept with vague 'natural' claims would get a fast, confident low score. Ambiguous concepts would get a hedged 3/5 with a note to research more.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would respond differently to the same ad depending on information richness. An ad with ingredient callouts, clinical claims, and a clear price point would drive immediate engagement and potential purchase. A purely aesthetic or emotional ad would be saved/screenshotted for later but wouldn't convert immediately. Urgency cues wouldn't feel manipulative but also wouldn't be the deciding factor — product substance would. Would respond best to ads that combine aesthetic appeal with an ingredient breakdown overlay, essentially compressing his research phase into the ad itself.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Masculinity Framing Sensitivity in Personal Care

selected_question.question_text: You're browsing body washes online and see three options with nearly identical ingredients and price (~₹350). Brand A has dark charcoal-black packaging, says 'Engineered for Men's Skin,' and shows a rugged male model. Brand B has clean white minimalist packaging, says 'Advanced Skin Science for All,' with no gendered imagery. Brand C has soft pastel packaging with botanical illustrations, says 'Gentle Nourishing Care,' and is marketed primarily toward women but has great reviews from men too. Which one ends up in your cart, and why? Would you feel weird about picking any of them?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the full spectrum of masculinity framing sensitivity by presenting three identical-ingredient products differentiated only by gendered semiotics — packaging color, language, imagery, and target audience. It's perfectly calibrated for this person because his profile contains genuine tension: he articulated wanting a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' (Q49) yet rates gender-neutral brands like Minimalist and Plum highest (Q48: 5), uses Dove and Pears (feminine-coded), prefers minimalist packaging (Q41), and values ingredients over branding (Q11). The question forces him to reveal whether gendered cues are affirming, irrelevant, or even off-putting — and the 'would you feel weird' follow-up captures social comfort with crossing gender lines. None of the 291 bank questions address gendered positioning; they focus on price/deal mechanics.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd go with Brand A honestly. If the ingredients are the same, I want something that feels like it was made for me — the dark packaging and 'for men's skin' thing just makes me feel like it understands what my skin actually needs. I wouldn't pick Brand C, that would feel odd in my bathroom. Brand B is fine but a bit generic — when I see 'for men' it signals they've thought about oiliness and sweat which are my actual concerns.
archetypes.why_plausible: He explicitly described wanting a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' in his ideal body wash (Q49), follows male actors and male beauty influencers (Q55), and his skin concerns are oiliness and acne (Q31) which he could associate with male-specific formulation. His preference for products that address India-specific issues like sweating and oiliness (Q52) could extend to gender-specific formulation claims.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a body wash concept with dark packaging, 'Built for Indian Men's Skin' tagline, and charcoal/wood imagery significantly higher on purchase intent. Would find a unisex Minimalist-style concept less compelling despite similar ingredients — would perceive it as less targeted to his needs. Would dismiss a soft/botanical concept as 'not for me.'
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would respond strongly to ads featuring a young man's active lifestyle — gym, cricket, going out — with a confident male voiceover and messaging around 'engineered for what men's skin actually faces.' Would find a gender-neutral self-care ad (meditation, relaxation, soft lighting) less engaging and less personally relevant. Would scroll past a feminine-coded influencer ad.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: medium — indifferent to gendered framing, prioritizes substance
archetypes.answer_text: Brand B, easy pick. I don't really care if it says 'for men' or not — I care about what's in it. The minimalist packaging with clean ingredient info is exactly what I look for. Brand A feels like they're compensating with the 'engineered for men' stuff instead of letting the product speak for itself. Brand C I wouldn't mind using if the reviews were good, but the botanical aesthetic doesn't tell me much about what it actually does for oily skin.
archetypes.why_plausible: His top purchase factors are expert opinion and ingredients over everything else (Q11). He prefers minimalist packaging (Q41), rates Minimalist and Plum (gender-neutral brands) as 5/5 while rating explicitly masculine brands like Beardo at 2/5 and The Man Company at 4/5 (Q48). He evaluates brands by checking 'if their claims resonate' (Q15) rather than by gendered positioning. His actual usage includes Dove and Pears — traditionally feminine-coded brands he chose without apparent discomfort.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a clean, ingredient-forward concept with clinical backing highest regardless of gendered framing. Would find a 'for men' concept slightly gimmicky — not off-putting but not a positive signal either. Would evaluate all concepts primarily on ingredient list, clinical claims, and sulphate-free credentials rather than packaging aesthetics or gendered language.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ads that lead with ingredient breakdowns, dermatologist endorsements, or before/after skin results — regardless of whether the model is male or female. Would find a hyper-masculine gym ad shallow and a soft self-care ad irrelevant. Would respond best to an aesthetic, informational Instagram reel showing formulation science, consistent with his preference for 'ingredient breakdown' and 'aesthetic content' (Q59).

archetypes.position_on_dimension: low — finds masculine framing limiting and performative
archetypes.answer_text: Brand B for sure, and I'd honestly consider Brand C too if the reviews backed it up. Brand A is a hard pass — 'Engineered for Men's Skin' is such a marketing gimmick. Beardo and those brands do the same thing and their products are mediocre. I've used Dove and Pears my whole life and they work fine. I'd rather pick something based on what actually works for my skin than buy into some macho packaging that's just selling an image. Wouldn't feel weird about Brand C at all — good skincare is good skincare.
archetypes.why_plausible: He rated Beardo 2/5 and The Man Company only 4/5 while giving Plum, Minimalist, and Dot & Key (all gender-neutral or feminine-leaning) perfect 5/5 scores (Q48). He grew up in a household where his mother and sister are particular about skincare (Q32), normalizing engagement with traditionally feminine personal care discourse. He trusts packaging claims the least (Q18: 1/5), suggesting skepticism toward surface-level positioning. He values small-scale innovative brands over large corporations (Q14), and hyper-masculine branding is typically associated with large corporate plays.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would actively penalize concepts with strong masculine codes — dark packaging, 'for men' language, and rugged imagery would lower purchase intent. Would rate a unisex, ingredient-forward concept highest. Could even respond positively to a softer, botanical concept if it demonstrated clinical efficacy and had good ingredient credentials. Would see masculine framing as a signal of style-over-substance.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would find hyper-masculine ads (gym, sports, 'man up' energy) cringe-worthy and a signal of a brand that prioritizes image over formulation. Would respond most positively to ads featuring relatable young men in everyday contexts (home, college) discussing skin concerns honestly — or even mixed-gender ads focused on skin science. Would share or engage with content that breaks stereotypes about men's grooming.
